calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR COURT
Off Calendar Nov-04-2025 Set For Trial On Aug-24-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Request For Production, To Compel Production, To Deem All Objections Waived, For Monetary Sanctions From Plaintiff Michael Zerbe And Plaintiff'S Counsel Of Record, And For Issue/Evidentiary Sanctions
Off Calendar Apr-27-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
The Court states a late notice to contest the Court's Tentative Ruling was received last Saturday. There are No appearances in Open Court. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT JEFFREY NEUSTADT , DBA LAW OFFICES OF JEFFRY B. NEUSTADT Motion To Compel Responses To Request For Production, To Compel Production, To Deem All Objections Waived, For Monetary Sanctions From Plaintiff Michael Zerbe And Plaintiff'S Counsel Of Record, And For Issue/Evidentiary Sanctions.

The motion of Defendant Jeffrey Neustadt to compel responses to compel responses to requests for production (Set 2), to compel production, to deem objections waived, and for monetary sanctions is GRANTED in part and DENIED in part.

(see word minutes for full details)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard C. Darwin, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RCD)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Special Interrogatories, To Deem All Objections Waived, And For Monetary Sanctions Form Plaintiff'S Counsel Of Record
Off Calendar Feb-18-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Jeffrey B. Neustadt's Motion To Compel Responses To Special Interrogatories, To Deem All Objections Waived, And For Monetary Sanctions Form Plaintiff's Counsel Of Record is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART.

The discovery requests at issue appear reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (See Code of Civil Procedure section 2017.010.) Plaintiff has not established that he provided timely responses to the interrogatories. No meet and confer requirement exists for motions to compel responses for Interrogatories when no responses have been provided. (Code of Civil Procedure section 2030.290.) Plaintiffs, however, met and conferred.

Plaintiff however provided responses and amended responses after Defendant filed his motion. The responses and amended responses appear to comply materially with the relevant Code provisions. And, contrary to Defendant's representation in his reply, Plaintiff's responses and amended responses are free of any material objection. On this record, the court declines to compel responses and the request to order objections waived is denied as moot.

Defendant contends that Plaintiff's responses are inadequate and/or non-compliant. The court finds the responses appear adequate and compliant. Any request to compel further responses must be made in an appropriately supported motion.

The court does not find Plaintiff Michael Zerbe and counsel Peter Johnson and Law Offices of Johnson & Johnson acted with substantial justification when they failed to provide timely responses. Plaintiff Michael Zerbe and counsel Peter Johnson and Law Offices of Johnson & Johnson, jointly and severally, shall pay to Defendant Jeffrey B. Neustadt, $110 as sanctions, payment no later than 15 court days after notice of this order. (See Code of Civil Procedure sections 2023.010, 2023.030, 2030.290(c).)

Order is signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

Off Calendar Nov-04-2025 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Oct-02-2025 Set For Trial On Jul-20-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Aug-18-2025 Continued To Oct-29-25 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-11-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Jeffrey Neustadt's Motion to Strike is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART.

A party may move to strike under CCP Section 436 "any irrelevant, false, or improper matter inserted in any pleading" or "all or any part of any pleading not drawn or filed in conformity with the laws of this state, a court rule, or an order of the court." "In the construction of a pleading, for the purpose of determining its effect, its allegations must be liberally construed, with a view to substantial justice between the parties." (CCP Section 452.)

Defendant's request for an order striking Plaintiff's request for attorney's fees is well taken. Under the "American rule," a prevailing party is not entitled to recover its attorney's fees from the losing party. (See Traveler's Cas. & Sur. Co. of Am. v. PG&E Co. (2007) 549 US 443.) Attorney's fees, however, may be awarded if authorized by contract or statute. (Code Civ. Proc., 1033.5, subds. (a)(10)(A) & (a)(10)(B).) This is an attorney malpractice case and the attorney's fees incurred successfully prosecuting such an action are generally not recoverable, at least not unless the claims are based on a contract that includes a fees provision. Without identifying a statutory or contractual basis, Plaintiff has requested "[a]ttorney's [f]ees required to recover damages due to defendant's negligence," or attorney's fees incurred in successfully prosecuting this action. Plaintiff has not identified a statute or contract that would allow their recovery here. Thus, his request for a prevailing party attorney's fees award is improper and, for that reason, stricken.

Plaintiff cites Oasis West realty, LLC v. Goldman (2011) 51 Cal.4th 811, for the proposition that fees incurred as a direct result of another's negligence are recoverable as damages. Yes, if Plaintiff proves that Defendant's negligence resulted in him incurring attorney's fees, say for obtaining relief from a default, then the fees may recoverable as special damages (which Plaintiff has separately pled). Oasis West, however, does not confer upon a successful malpractice plaintiff a right to recover prevailing party fees. Nothing the court is saying here should be construed to disallow Plaintiff from recovering fees that are properly regarded as special damages under Oasis West and Jordache.

Defendant's request for an order striking Plaintiff's general damages claim is on less steady ground. Under the case-within-a-case standard, a malpractice plaintiff who proves he would have obtained a more favorable result but for the attorney's negligence may recover for losses, including emotional distress, actually caused by the attorney's breach. (See, e.g., Mattco Forge, Inc. v. Arthur Young & Co. (1997) 52 Cal.App.4th 820, 834.) It is true emotional distress damages are often not supported under the case-within-a-case standard, but they may be recoverable where such injury "naturally ensues from the acts complained of." (Se, e.g., Merenda v. Superior Court (1992) 3 Cal.App.4th 1, 6, disapproved on other grounds in Ferguson v. Lieff, Cabraser, Heimann & Bernstein (2003) 30 Cal.4th 1037.) The court cannot say on this record that Plaintiff's request for general damages is necessarily improper.

Plaintiff's request for a prevailing party attorney's fees award is stricken and his request for general damages stands.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the court's ruling and must drop a hard courtesy copy in Dept. 302's inbox.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: M. Macadangdang; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
